## vvx
Job Jockey Kit - MCP tools for project initiative management

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

Docket:
  ## Character
  
  Verify the iterative-debug surface of the gauntlet suite, sequenced after BBAAg (suite + rbw-tP exist) and before BBAAX (first end-to-end run, green-tally guardrail). BBAAX commits to marshal-zero-between-attempts — without proven single-case rerun against the suite's expensive state-progressing cases (especially §3 ordain, where vessel construction is the costliest step), each debug iteration costs ~1 hour + a GCP project, and the gauntlet's value collapses to "must get it perfect in one shot." That contradicts BBAAX's first-class re-run clause.
  
  Intent-only docket. Code-level planning at mount time.
  
  ## Scope
  
  - Verify (or add) `tt/rbtd-s.SingleCase.canonical-onboarding-sequence.sh` handles ordain-* cases against an already-canonical depot, no upstream re-run
  - Tamper deliberately — delete a hallmark, blank a regime field, leave a partial reliquary — and verify per-case precondition probes fire with operator-actionable diagnostics naming the expected state and the closest fixture that produces it
  - Confirm §4 crucible fixtures (tadmor/moriah/srjcl/pluml) runnable alone against a §3-completed state, no re-ordain required for crucible debug
  - Document the iterative debug workflow into the gauntlet runbook surface BBAAI owns: where to look when X fails, what tabtarget to invoke, how to interpret per-case probe output
  
  ## Wrap criterion
  
  - Single-case rerun mechanism demonstrably works for ordain-* cases without marshal-zero, against established canonical state
  - Per-case probes verified under deliberately-broken state across at least one ordain case and one crucible fixture
  - §4 crucible single-fixture rerun confirmed against §3-completed depot
  - Debug workflow referenced from BBAAX's docket so the green-tally pace has tooling on hand
  
  ## Cost
  
  ~30-45 min wall-clock. Reuses canonical state from a prior end-to-end run; does not require its own marshal-zero + GCP project.
  
  ## Cross-pace dependencies
  
  - BBAAf landed (canonical-onboarding-sequence fixture + per-case probes exist)
  - BBAAg landed (rbw-tP + suite resolver exist)
  
  ## Out of scope
  
  - New mechanism design — per-case probe pattern is BBAAf's territory; this pace verifies behavior, not invents it
  - BBAAX's first end-to-end green-tally run (uses the tooling proven here)
  - Cross-heat debug tooling generalizations

1	# jjezs_paddock ₣BB
2	
3	## Context
4	
5	Release qualification gap surfaced by the ₣A_ depot-regen heat. Every existing test tier (`rbw-tf` fast, `rbw-tr` release) tolerates accumulated depot state, so silent first-build assumptions slip through. Recent spooks — kludge-aware-charge-prereq (k-prefixed hallmarks at GAR), ZRBOB_PROJECT (compose project name without runtime prefix), rbob_charged_predicate prefix omission, reliquary integrity-broken on negative test — all share a property: they only manifest on first-build paths, and none were caught until live verification surfaced them.
6	
7	This heat constructs `rbw-tP` QualifyPristine — a third tier that **refuses to run** unless marshal-zero state was just committed. The refusal is enforced by the test itself, not by ceremony or operator discipline.
8	
9	## Single operator prerequisite chain
10	
11	Operator has exactly one prerequisite chain: confirm Payor health → marshal-zero → commit → run `rbw-tP`. Payor OAuth is the only credential the operator must have ready; everything else (governor, retriever, director SAs and their RBRA files) is **minted by the qualification itself**, not restored from backup.
12	
13	## Entry contract — load-bearing
14	
15	`rbw-tP` fails-fast unless ALL of the following hold:
16	
17	- Working tree clean
18	- HEAD commit is a marshal-zero commit (detectable signature baked into `rblm_zero` per BBAAB)
19	- RBRR fields are blank (prefixes, depot project ID, etc.)
20	- No RBRA credential files present (governor, retriever, director — all deleted by marshal-zero, recreated by the qualification)
21	- No hallmark fields populated in nameplates
22	- No depot-scoped fields populated in vessel rbrv.env files
23	
24	Operator cannot skip the prerequisite. This is the property that makes the tier catch the silent-first-build bug class by construction.
25	
26	## Failure mode contract
27	
28	Mid-qualification failure means start-over-from-zero, not patch-and-continue. Documented explicitly in runbook to prevent the very accumulated-state bug class this tier exists to catch.
29	
30	## Release-branch execution contract
31	
32	Three properties bind every fixture and phase in this tier.
33	
34	**Run on a release branch.** The release machinery is engineered for branch execution, not main. The branch will accumulate many commits across a successful pristine run. After a failed run, the branch holds bygone commits that `rbw-MZ` does not touch — they remain as history; the next run starts from a fresh marshal-zero commit on top.
35	
36	**Commits during the run are first-class.** Each step that mutates regime state (rbrr.env, rbra files, vessel rbrv.env, etc.) commits the change. Container-recipe work has a documented cognitive failure mode: humans lose track of where they are in long sequences. The growing commit trail is the operator's mental anchor against that.
37	
38	**Stop on very first failure, cleanly and clearly.** A step fails → fixture stops → operator stops → debugging happens immediately on the failed branch. Stderr names the failed step; fixture exits non-zero. Recovery is `rbw-MZ` + retry on a fresh marshal-zero. No graceful degradation, no recovery branches.
39	
40	Engineering scaffolding for any other shape — partial-run cleanup, soft-delete tolerance, multi-mode failure handling, recovery-time orphan inspection, diagnostic-redundancy — is non-load-bearing and does not belong in pristine-tier code. The single mechanical path is the entire surface.
41	
42	## Tier layering
43	
44	Three tiers, escalating cost:
45	
46	| Colophon | Frontispiece | Cost | Entry contract |
47	|----------|--------------|------|----------------|
48	| `rbw-tf` | QualifyFast | seconds | none |
49	| `rbw-tr` | QualifyRelease | minutes | none (accumulated state OK) |
50	| `rbw-tP` | QualifyPristine *(new)* | ~1 hour + cloud $ | marshal-zero just committed |
51	
52	`rbw-tP` is THE release gate. `rbw-tr` becomes the pre-pristine smoke test — cheap-and-frequent for development confidence; pristine for actual release.
53	
54	## Gauntlet test suite — design
55	
56	`rbw-tP` exec's the **gauntlet suite** — a TestSuite (not a custom orchestrator script) composed of fixtures sequenced from marshal-zero state through canonical-credentialed state to crucible verification. The TestSuite mechanism is existing infrastructure (`tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.{fast,service,crucible,complete}.sh`); the gauntlet suite extends it.
57	
58	**Suite-of-fixtures, not orchestrator-script.** The original construction plan (custom bash orchestrator with inline tabtarget calls) was abandoned in favor of suite composition. Each section of the release-qualification sequence becomes a fixture; the suite composes them in order.
59	
60	**Naming hygiene.** "Gauntlet" is the suite name; "pristine" is reserved for the §1 pristine-lifecycle fixture and informally for tier vocabulary. The suite name does not appear at fixture or case level — distinct stratification across naming layers (suite ≠ fixture ≠ case).
61	
62	**State ladder, fixtures along it.** The suite walks a state ladder no single fixture covers:
63	
64	1. enrollment-validation (preflight, state-indifferent)
65	2. pristine-lifecycle (§1: marshal-zero gate + throwaway depot/SA lifecycle)
66	3. canonical-establish (§2: canonical depot levy + governor mantle + retriever invest + director invest + IAM propagation wait)
67	4. canonical-onboarding-sequence (§3: reliquary inscribe + per-vessel hallmark ordain — handbook-driven build walks per BBAAf)
68	5. regime-validation, regime-smoke (post-§3, regimes now populated)
69	6. four-mode (cloud-build mode coverage)
70	7. tadmor, moriah, srjcl, pluml (§4: per-crucible test fixtures, vessel-exists preconditions per BBAAs)
71	
72	No automatic teardown — see "No-teardown decision" below.
73	
74	## Dual purpose: handbook-regression coverage
75	
76	The gauntlet's primary purpose is release qualification (catch silent first-build assumptions). Its **secondary purpose** is automated handbook-regression coverage: ensure operator-facing onboarding handbook tracks (`rbh*`) stay in sync with working code so future operators following the prescribed steps aren't surprised by drift.
77	
78	Build-side handbook coverage lives in BBAAf (canonical-onboarding-sequence): each ordain case walks the corresponding director-mode handbook track (`rbw-Odf` conjure / `rbw-Oda` airgap / `rbw-Odb` bind / `rbw-Odg` graft) rather than calling inscribe/ordain machinery directly.
79	
80	**Build/run split — load-bearing.** Handbook walks cover the BUILD step (vessel construction) and stop when the hallmark lands in GAR. Run-side fixtures (per-crucible: tadmor/moriah/srjcl/pluml) stay as direct test fixtures with vessel-exists preconditions, NOT handbook walks. The split keeps run-side reusable against any vessel source — coupling them to handbook prose breaks reusability.
81	
82	Drift between fixtures and handbooks is accepted future maintenance: whoever changes one is responsible for updating the other; the gauntlet's job is to surface drift, not prevent it by construction.
83	
84	Run-side handbook coverage (`rbw-Ots`, the rack portion of `rbw-Ofc`) is explicitly out of scope for this heat.
85	
86	## Fixture disposition — Independent vs StateProgressing
87	
88	Each fixture declares a disposition flag in Rust, controlling engine behavior:
89	
90	- **Independent** — cases are self-contained; suite-order is informational. Engine permits keep-going mode for surveying.
91	- **StateProgressing** — case N's success establishes preconditions for case N+1. Engine refuses keep-going mode (incoherent: failed case leaves broken precondition). Fail-fast required.
92	
93	**Default is Independent.** Existing fixtures (`enrollment-validation`, `regime-validation`, `regime-smoke`, `four-mode`, `tadmor`, `moriah`, `srjcl`, `pluml`) are Independent and require no tagging change. Only fixtures whose case-N success establishes case-N+1 preconditions get StateProgressing — currently `pristine-lifecycle`, `canonical-establish`, and `canonical-onboarding-sequence`.
94	
95	Suite-level disposition derives from membership: any StateProgressing fixture in the suite makes the suite state-progressing → suite-level keep-going refused.
96	
97	**Per-case precondition probes.** Each case in a StateProgressing fixture probes its precondition at start. If state matches expectation, run. If not, refuse with operator-actionable diagnostic naming the expected state and the closest fixture that produces it. This enables safe a-la-carte single-case rerun — the engine doesn't need to know the case's history; the case's probe enforces.
98	
99	## Suite-level fail-fast
100	
101	Across all suites — not just gauntlet — any fixture failure stops subsequent fixtures from running automatically. State-progressing suites require this for correctness; independent suites adopt it for the simpler mental model. Operators who want full surveying run individual fixtures separately.
102	
103	## No-teardown decision
104	
105	The gauntlet suite omits an automatic teardown phase. After green tally, the canonical depot, SAs, hallmarks, and RBRA files persist for operator inspection. Deliberate trade-off:
106	
107	- **Inspect-after-success** is genuine operator value — browse canonical depot in GCP console, inspect GAR contents, run ad-hoc verification a test sequence cannot anticipate.
108	- **Cleanup is operator-driven** — manual sequence is `rbw-fA` per vessel + `rbw-arD` + `rbw-adD` + `rbw-dU` (governor cleanup folds into unmake per BBAAN). All existing tabtargets; no new infrastructure.
109	- **Failed runs don't accumulate cleanup debt** — under fail-fast, teardown wouldn't run anyway; the no-teardown decision only changes behavior on full success.
110	- **Cost visibility** — pending-delete soft-deleted projects accumulate per run (RELEASE.md accepts this); not changed by teardown decision.
111	
112	Documentation of the post-success cleanup ceremony is BBAAI work (RELEASE.md).
113	
114	## Operator scope
115	
116	Single operator (project lead). Not designed for multi-operator workflow. The README.md release section remains the runbook for the gauntlet itself; the dual-purpose handbook-regression coverage protects the broader operator surface (onboarding tracks under `rbh*`) from drift.
117	
118	## Coupling to ₣A_
119	
120	Cutover work from ₣A_ informing `rbw-tP`'s sequence has landed (BBAAM depot-identity-collapse). ₣A_'s remaining paces run independently of BB.
121	
122	## Locked design decisions
123	
124	- **Depot identity collapse**: RBRR collapses to `RBRR_DEPOT_MONIKER`; `RBRR_CLOUD_PREFIX` flows through depot-affiliated resources; per-moniker depot fact-files; pristine-fixture moniker autodetect lives in Rust (`rbtdrp_pristine.rs`), not payor.
125	- **Cult-verb naming**: SA domain muster→**roster**; image domain muster→**audit**. Domain-exclusive split; lowercase tail letters preserved for no-cloud-change observation colophons.
126	- **Build/run split**: handbook walks cover build only; per-crucible fixtures stay reusable as direct tests.
127	
128	## Calibrant fixture family — BBAAh
129	
130	Synthetic Rust fixtures with deterministic verdicts, registered in the manifest by BBAAh. The family exists to give ₣BC `rbk-mvp-3-theurge-self-test` deterministic targets for bash-blackbox driving and manifest-coverage validation; here in ₣BB only the foundation lands.
131	
132	**Anchor word: `calibrant`.** Module `rbtdrl_calibrant.rs` (classifier `l` for caLibrant since `c` is owned by crucible). Replaces the dormant `rbtdrd_dummy.rs` placeholder. Test counterpart `rbtdtl_calibrant.rs`. Internal-only vocabulary; not end-user-facing.
133	
134	**Fixture catalog** (Rust, manifest-registered):
135	
136	- `calibrant-verdicts` (Independent) — pass / fail / skip / pass_with_output. Verdict-path coverage. The case-written file (`output.txt`) is distinct from the engine-auto `trace.txt`.
137	- `calibrant-fail-fast` (Independent, multi-section) — fixture-internal fail-fast across cases and across sections; "not_reached" cases write sentinel files used as absence-of-side-effect assertions.
138	- `calibrant-progressing` (StateProgressing) — exercises `rbtdrb_Probe` diagnostic format and `rbtdre_resolve_fail_fast` policy gate.
139	- `calibrant-sentinel` (Independent) — single case writing a known file when it runs. Used for suite-level fail-fast verification.
140	
141	Suite: `calibrant` = [verdicts, fail-fast, sentinel]. Sentinel-after-fail verifies cross-fixture fail-fast contract.
142	
143	Downstream consumption — bash testbench, manifest-coverage runtime checks, coverage-validation fixtures and driver — lives in ₣BC.
144	
145	## References
146	
147	- ₣A_ rbk-mvp-3-resource-prefix-and-depot-regen — surfaced the gap
148	- ₣BC rbk-mvp-3-theurge-self-test — calibrant downstream consumers
149	- `tt/rbw-tr.QualifyRelease.sh` — current release qualify; layered alongside, not replaced
150	- `Tools/rbk/rblm_cli.sh` — `rbw-MZ` zeroes local regime; marshal-zero signature baked here per BBAAB
151	- `.claude/commands/rbk-prep-release.md` — upstream contribution ceremony; pristine-pass becomes a precondition (BBAAH)
152	
153	# jjezs_pace ₢BBAAr
154	
155	## Character
156	
157	Verify the iterative-debug surface of the gauntlet suite, sequenced after BBAAg (suite + rbw-tP exist) and before BBAAX (first end-to-end run, green-tally guardrail). BBAAX commits to marshal-zero-between-attempts — without proven single-case rerun against the suite's expensive state-progressing cases (especially §3 ordain, where vessel construction is the costliest step), each debug iteration costs ~1 hour + a GCP project, and the gauntlet's value collapses to "must get it perfect in one shot." That contradicts BBAAX's first-class re-run clause.
158	
159	Intent-only docket. Code-level planning at mount time.
160	
161	## Scope
162	
163	- Verify (or add) `tt/rbtd-s.SingleCase.canonical-onboarding-sequence.sh` handles ordain-* cases against an already-canonical depot, no upstream re-run
164	- Tamper deliberately — delete a hallmark, blank a regime field, leave a partial reliquary — and verify per-case precondition probes fire with operator-actionable diagnostics naming the expected state and the closest fixture that produces it
165	- Confirm §4 crucible fixtures (tadmor/moriah/srjcl/pluml) runnable alone against a §3-completed state, no re-ordain required for crucible debug
166	- Document the iterative debug workflow into the gauntlet runbook surface BBAAI owns: where to look when X fails, what tabtarget to invoke, how to interpret per-case probe output
167	
168	## Wrap criterion
169	
170	- Single-case rerun mechanism demonstrably works for ordain-* cases without marshal-zero, against established canonical state
171	- Per-case probes verified under deliberately-broken state across at least one ordain case and one crucible fixture
172	- §4 crucible single-fixture rerun confirmed against §3-completed depot
173	- Debug workflow referenced from BBAAX's docket so the green-tally pace has tooling on hand
174	
175	## Cost
176	
177	~30-45 min wall-clock. Reuses canonical state from a prior end-to-end run; does not require its own marshal-zero + GCP project.
178	
179	## Cross-pace dependencies
180	
181	- BBAAf landed (canonical-onboarding-sequence fixture + per-case probes exist)
182	- BBAAg landed (rbw-tP + suite resolver exist)
183	
184	## Out of scope
185	
186	- New mechanism design — per-case probe pattern is BBAAf's territory; this pace verifies behavior, not invents it
187	- BBAAX's first end-to-end green-tally run (uses the tooling proven here)
188	- Cross-heat debug tooling generalizations
189

Mid-qualification failure means start-over-from-zero, not patch-and-continue. Documented explicitly in runbook to prevent the very accumulated-state bug class this tier exists to catch.

## Release-branch execution contract

Three properties bind every fixture and phase in this tier.

**Run on a release branch.** The release machinery is engineered for branch execution, not main. The branch will accumulate many commits across a successful pristine run. After a failed run, the branch holds bygone commits that `rbw-MZ` does not touch — they remain as history; the next run starts from a fresh marshal-zero commit on top.

**Commits during the run are first-class.** Each step that mutates regime state (rbrr.env, rbra files, vessel rbrv.env, etc.) commits the change. Container-recipe work has a documented cognitive failure mode: humans lose track of where they are in long sequences. The growing commit trail is the operator's mental anchor against that.

**Stop on very first failure, cleanly and clearly.** A step fails → fixture stops → operator stops → debugging happens immediately on the failed branch. Stderr names the failed step; fixture exits non-zero. Recovery is `rbw-MZ` + retry on a fresh marshal-zero. No graceful degradation, no recovery branches.

Engineering scaffolding for any other shape — partial-run cleanup, soft-delete tolerance, multi-mode failure handling, recovery-time orphan inspection, diagnostic-redundancy — is non-load-bearing and does not belong in pristine-tier code. The single mechanical path is the entire surface.

## Tier layering

Three tiers, escalating cost:

| Colophon | Frontispiece | Cost | Entry contract |
|----------|--------------|------|----------------|
| `rbw-tf` | QualifyFast | seconds | none |
| `rbw-tr` | QualifyRelease | minutes | none (accumulated state OK) |
| `rbw-tP` | QualifyPristine *(new)* | ~1 hour + cloud $ | marshal-zero just committed |

`rbw-tP` is THE release gate. `rbw-tr` becomes the pre-pristine smoke test — cheap-and-frequent for development confidence; pristine for actual release.

## Gauntlet test suite — design

`rbw-tP` exec's the **gauntlet suite** — a TestSuite (not a custom orchestrator script) composed of fixtures sequenced from marshal-zero state through canonical-credentialed state to crucible verification. The TestSuite mechanism is existing infrastructure (`tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.{fast,service,crucible,complete}.sh`); the gauntlet suite extends it.

**Suite-of-fixtures, not orchestrator-script.** The original construction plan (custom bash orchestrator with inline tabtarget calls) was abandoned in favor of suite composition. Each section of the release-qualification sequence becomes a fixture; the suite composes them in order.

**Naming hygiene.** "Gauntlet" is the suite name; "pristine" is reserved for the §1 pristine-lifecycle fixture and informally for tier vocabulary. The suite name does not appear at fixture or case level — distinct stratification across naming layers (suite ≠ fixture ≠ case).

**State ladder, fixtures along it.** The suite walks a state ladder no single fixture covers:

1. enrollment-validation (preflight, state-indifferent)
2. pristine-lifecycle (§1: marshal-zero gate + throwaway depot/SA lifecycle)
3. canonical-establish (§2: canonical depot levy + governor mantle + retriever invest + director invest + IAM propagation wait)
4. canonical-onboarding-sequence (§3: reliquary inscribe + per-vessel hallmark ordain — handbook-driven build walks per BBAAf)
5. regime-validation, regime-smoke (post-§3, regimes now populated)
6. four-mode (cloud-build mode coverage)
7. tadmor, moriah, srjcl, pluml (§4: per-crucible test fixtures, vessel-exists preconditions per BBAAs)

No automatic teardown — see "No-teardown decision" below.

## Dual purpose: handbook-regression coverage

The gauntlet's primary purpose is release qualification (catch silent first-build assumptions). Its **secondary purpose** is automated handbook-regression coverage: ensure operator-facing onboarding handbook tracks (`rbh*`) stay in sync with working code so future operators following the prescribed steps aren't surprised by drift.

Build-side handbook coverage lives in BBAAf (canonical-onboarding-sequence): each ordain case walks the corresponding director-mode handbook track (`rbw-Odf` conjure / `rbw-Oda` airgap / `rbw-Odb` bind / `rbw-Odg` graft) rather than calling inscribe/ordain machinery directly.

**Build/run split — load-bearing.** Handbook walks cover the BUILD step (vessel construction) and stop when the hallmark lands in GAR. Run-side fixtures (per-crucible: tadmor/moriah/srjcl/pluml) stay as direct test fixtures with vessel-exists preconditions, NOT handbook walks. The split keeps run-side reusable against any vessel source — coupling them to handbook prose breaks reusability.

Drift between fixtures and handbooks is accepted future maintenance: whoever changes one is responsible for updating the other; the gauntlet's job is to surface drift, not prevent it by construction.

Run-side handbook coverage (`rbw-Ots`, the rack portion of `rbw-Ofc`) is explicitly out of scope for this heat.

## Fixture disposition — Independent vs StateProgressing

Each fixture declares a disposition flag in Rust, controlling engine behavior:

- **Independent** — cases are self-contained; suite-order is informational. Engine permits keep-going mode for surveying.
- **StateProgressing** — case N's success establishes preconditions for case N+1. Engine refuses keep-going mode (incoherent: failed case leaves broken precondition). Fail-fast required.

**Default is Independent.** Existing fixtures (`enrollment-validation`, `regime-validation`, `regime-smoke`, `four-mode`, `tadmor`, `moriah`, `srjcl`, `pluml`) are Independent and require no tagging change. Only fixtures whose case-N success establishes case-N+1 preconditions get StateProgressing — currently `pristine-lifecycle`, `canonical-establish`, and `canonical-onboarding-sequence`.

Suite-level disposition derives from membership: any StateProgressing fixture in the suite makes the suite state-progressing → suite-level keep-going refused.

**Per-case precondition probes.** Each case in a StateProgressing fixture probes its precondition at start. If state matches expectation, run. If not, refuse with operator-actionable diagnostic naming the expected state and the closest fixture that produces it. This enables safe a-la-carte single-case rerun — the engine doesn't need to know the case's history; the case's probe enforces.

## Suite-level fail-fast

Across all suites — not just gauntlet — any fixture failure stops subsequent fixtures from running automatically. State-progressing suites require this for correctness; independent suites adopt it for the simpler mental model. Operators who want full surveying run individual fixtures separately.

## No-teardown decision

The gauntlet suite omits an automatic teardown phase. After green tally, the canonical depot, SAs, hallmarks, and RBRA files persist for operator inspection. Deliberate trade-off:

- **Inspect-after-success** is genuine operator value — browse canonical depot in GCP console, inspect GAR contents, run ad-hoc verification a test sequence cannot anticipate.
- **Cleanup is operator-driven** — manual sequence is `rbw-fA` per vessel + `rbw-arD` + `rbw-adD` + `rbw-dU` (governor cleanup folds into unmake per BBAAN). All existing tabtargets; no new infrastructure.
- **Failed runs don't accumulate cleanup debt** — under fail-fast, teardown wouldn't run anyway; the no-teardown decision only changes behavior on full success.
- **Cost visibility** — pending-delete soft-deleted projects accumulate per run (RELEASE.md accepts this); not changed by teardown decision.

Documentation of the post-success cleanup ceremony is BBAAI work (RELEASE.md).

## Operator scope

Single operator (project lead). Not designed for multi-operator workflow. The README.md release section remains the runbook for the gauntlet itself; the dual-purpose handbook-regression coverage protects the broader operator surface (onboarding tracks under `rbh*`) from drift.

## Coupling to ₣A_

Cutover work from ₣A_ informing `rbw-tP`'s sequence has landed (BBAAM depot-identity-collapse). ₣A_'s remaining paces run independently of BB.

## Locked design decisions

- **Depot identity collapse**: RBRR collapses to `RBRR_DEPOT_MONIKER`; `RBRR_CLOUD_PREFIX` flows through depot-affiliated resources; per-moniker depot fact-files; pristine-fixture moniker autodetect lives in Rust (`rbtdrp_pristine.rs`), not payor.
- **Cult-verb naming**: SA domain muster→**roster**; image domain muster→**audit**. Domain-exclusive split; lowercase tail letters preserved for no-cloud-change observation colophons.
- **Build/run split**: handbook walks cover build only; per-crucible fixtures stay reusable as direct tests.

## Calibrant fixture family — BBAAh

Synthetic Rust fixtures with deterministic verdicts, registered in the manifest by BBAAh. The family exists to give ₣BC `rbk-mvp-3-theurge-self-test` deterministic targets for bash-blackbox driving and manifest-coverage validation; here in ₣BB only the foundation lands.

**Anchor word: `calibrant`.** Module `rbtdrl_calibrant.rs` (classifier `l` for caLibrant since `c` is owned by crucible). Replaces the dormant `rbtdrd_dummy.rs` placeholder. Test counterpart `rbtdtl_calibrant.rs`. Internal-only vocabulary; not end-user-facing.

**Fixture catalog** (Rust, manifest-registered):

- `calibrant-verdicts` (Independent) — pass / fail / skip / pass_with_output. Verdict-path coverage. The case-written file (`output.txt`) is distinct from the engine-auto `trace.txt`.
- `calibrant-fail-fast` (Independent, multi-section) — fixture-internal fail-fast across cases and across sections; "not_reached" cases write sentinel files used as absence-of-side-effect assertions.
- `calibrant-progressing` (StateProgressing) — exercises `rbtdrb_Probe` diagnostic format and `rbtdre_resolve_fail_fast` policy gate.
- `calibrant-sentinel` (Independent) — single case writing a known file when it runs. Used for suite-level fail-fast verification.

Suite: `calibrant` = [verdicts, fail-fast, sentinel]. Sentinel-after-fail verifies cross-fixture fail-fast contract.

Downstream consumption — bash testbench, manifest-coverage runtime checks, coverage-validation fixtures and driver — lives in ₣BC.

## References

- ₣A_ rbk-mvp-3-resource-prefix-and-depot-regen — surfaced the gap
- ₣BC rbk-mvp-3-theurge-self-test — calibrant downstream consumers
- `tt/rbw-tr.QualifyRelease.sh` — current release qualify; layered alongside, not replaced
- `Tools/rbk/rblm_cli.sh` — `rbw-MZ` zeroes local regime; marshal-zero signature baked here per BBAAB
- `.claude/commands/rbk-prep-release.md` — upstream contribution ceremony; pristine-pass becomes a precondition (BBAAH)

# jjezs_pace ₢BBAAr

## Character

Verify the iterative-debug surface of the gauntlet suite, sequenced after BBAAg (suite + rbw-tP exist) and before BBAAX (first end-to-end run, green-tally guardrail). BBAAX commits to marshal-zero-between-attempts — without proven single-case rerun against the suite's expensive state-progressing cases (especially §3 ordain, where vessel construction is the costliest step), each debug iteration costs ~1 hour + a GCP project, and the gauntlet's value collapses to "must get it perfect in one shot." That contradicts BBAAX's first-class re-run clause.

Intent-only docket. Code-level planning at mount time.

## Scope

- Verify (or add) `tt/rbtd-s.SingleCase.canonical-onboarding-sequence.sh` handles ordain-* cases against an already-canonical depot, no upstream re-run
- Tamper deliberately — delete a hallmark, blank a regime field, leave a partial reliquary — and verify per-case precondition probes fire with operator-actionable diagnostics naming the expected state and the closest fixture that produces it
- Confirm §4 crucible fixtures (tadmor/moriah/srjcl/pluml) runnable alone against a §3-completed state, no re-ordain required for crucible debug
- Document the iterative debug workflow into the gauntlet runbook surface BBAAI owns: where to look when X fails, what tabtarget to invoke, how to interpret per-case probe output

## Wrap criterion

- Single-case rerun mechanism demonstrably works for ordain-* cases without marshal-zero, against established canonical state
- Per-case probes verified under deliberately-broken state across at least one ordain case and one crucible fixture
- §4 crucible single-fixture rerun confirmed against §3-completed depot
- Debug workflow referenced from BBAAX's docket so the green-tally pace has tooling on hand

## Cost

~30-45 min wall-clock. Reuses canonical state from a prior end-to-end run; does not require its own marshal-zero + GCP project.

## Cross-pace dependencies

- BBAAf landed (canonical-onboarding-sequence fixture + per-case probes exist)
- BBAAg landed (rbw-tP + suite resolver exist)

## Out of scope

- New mechanism design — per-case probe pattern is BBAAf's territory; this pace verifies behavior, not invents it
- BBAAX's first end-to-end green-tally run (uses the tooling proven here)
- Cross-heat debug tooling generalizations

content.text:
Reading it cold, with paddock loaded — I see a few tensions worth flagging before we mount.

**1. Setup-cost contradiction.** The docket says cost is "~30-45 min wall-clock. Reuses canonical state from a prior end-to-end run." But BBAAr is sequenced *before* BBAAX, which is itself the first end-to-end run. So unless you've informally walked the gauntlet by hand, there is no prior canonical state to reuse — BBAAr would have to run §1–§3 of the gauntlet itself, which is ~80% of BBAAX's cost.

**2. Substantial overlap with what BBAAX would surface anyway.** BBAAX is the green-tally guardrail run. If single-case rerun is broken, BBAAX discovers it the moment it fails on case 3 of an ordain-* fixture and you try to retry. BBAAr proposes to discover that ahead of BBAAX, but at nearly the same cost.

**3. Trust posture against BBAAf.** The dependency line says "BBAAf landed (canonical-onboarding-sequence fixture + per-case probes exist)." If BBAAf landed the probe pattern correctly, the rerun infrastructure should work by construction — `tt/rbtd-s.FixtureCase.{fixture}.sh` is already documented in CLAUDE.md as the single-case rerun mechanism. BBAAr is verification of an invariant that *should* be design-true, with empirical checks that themselves require expensive setup.

**4. Paddock-vs-docket subtle tension.** Paddock locked design says pristine *run* recovery is "`rbw-MZ` + retry on a fresh marshal-zero. No graceful degradation, no recovery branches." BBAAr is about *development/debug*-time rerun, which is a different concern — but worth being explicit about, because someone reading both could conflate them and conclude BBAAr is partial-run cleanup tooling.

**5. The documentation slice has independent value.** "Where to look when X fails, what tabtarget to invoke, how to interpret per-case probe output" is useful regardless of whether the verification slice happens — and it's cheap.

**My read:** The pace as written looks like an over-cautious dress rehearsal for BBAAX. Three plausible reshapes —
- **Collapse:** fold into BBAAX. Discover gaps in situ; only carve out a follow-up if the discovery is gnarly.
- **Scope down:** keep only the documentation slice; let BBAAX do the empirical verification.
- **Substitute target:** verify rerun against the *calibrant* fixtures (BBAAh) instead of real ordain cases — deterministic, cheap, mechanically faithful to the rerun engine. But this requires BBAAh to have landed and be sufficient as a proxy.

Want to dig into any of these before deciding mount/reslate/drop?

- supply chain — inscribe [Reliquary](README.md#Reliquary), [Ordain](README.md#Ordain) all [Hallmarks](README.md#Hallmark)
- [Crucible](README.md#Crucible) suite
- cleanup

Outcome:

- Success: green tally and clean exit
- Failure: return to step 2 — never patch-and-continue

```
tt/rbw-tP.QualifyPristine.sh
```

## 4. Prepare the upstream release

- Claude Code slash command, not a tabtarget — the contribution ceremony is interactive by design
- Drives the upstream contribution review and produces the contribution-ready branch
- Success: the ceremony reports a clean upstream-ready state
- Failure: address findings and re-run; if the underlying issue is structural, return to step 2

```

```

## 5. Push to the upstream remote

- Universal git operation; no tabtarget
- Pushes the contribution-ready branch produced by step 4
- Success: the upstream accepts the push
- Failure: resolve the upstream-side reason (branch protection, force-push policy, etc.) and retry — the local state from step 4 remains valid

```
git push <upstream> <branch>
```

content.text:
No — the alignment is inverted, not partial. Reading RELEASE.md against BBAAr surfaces a posture conflict.

**RELEASE.md's load-bearing contract (lines 10–11, 74):**
> "Mid-qualification failure means start over from step 2, not patch-and-continue. Patching forward is exactly the bug class this ceremony exists to catch — accumulated state hides first-build assumptions."
> "Failure: return to step 2 — never patch-and-continue"

The paddock echoes this verbatim ("Recovery is `rbw-MZ` + retry on a fresh marshal-zero. No graceful degradation, no recovery branches.") and explicitly closes the door on alternatives ("Engineering scaffolding for any other shape — partial-run cleanup, soft-delete tolerance, multi-mode failure handling … is non-load-bearing and does not belong in pristine-tier code.")

**BBAAr's scope (lines 163–166 of the docket):**
> "Verify (or add) `tt/rbtd-s.SingleCase.canonical-onboarding-sequence.sh` handles ordain-* cases against an already-canonical depot, no upstream re-run"
> "Confirm §4 crucible fixtures … runnable alone against a §3-completed state, no re-ordain required for crucible debug"

That is a workflow for resuming mid-gauntlet against accumulated canonical state — the precise shape RELEASE.md and the paddock both rule out. BBAAr's framing premise — "BBAAX commits to marshal-zero-between-attempts … the gauntlet's value collapses to 'must get it perfect in one shot.'" — is not a flaw to mitigate; it is the *design intent*. RELEASE.md embraces it: ~1 hour + two GCP projects per attempt is the accepted cost.

**Where the docket tries to thread the needle.** It hedges with "iterative debug" framing — implying the rerun infrastructure is for *Claude/operator development of the pace*, not for *operator release-ceremony recovery*. But:

1. The tooling it produces (`tt/rbtd-s.SingleCase.canonical-onboarding-sequence.sh`, "documented iterative debug workflow") cannot distinguish caller intent. Once it exists and is documented, operators will reach for it under failure.
2. The wrap criterion "Debug workflow referenced from BBAAX's docket so the green-tally pace has tooling on hand" reads as wiring this into the actual qualification path, not just internal pace development.
3. RELEASE.md was specifically written to make "return to step 2" non-negotiable. A reachable alternative path corrodes that.

**My read:** BBAAr was slated when the design was still drifting. RELEASE.md (which I expect post-dates BBAAr's slate) crystallized "no patch-forward" as load-bearing. The pace is now stale-by-mount in posture, not just in detail.

**Three options:**
- **Drop.** It contradicts contract; salvage nothing.
- **Reslate to documentation-only.** Keep "where to look when X fails, what tabtarget to invoke" — but the answer is "rbw-MZ; retry; if it fails twice in the same place, that's the bug to investigate." That's a paragraph in RELEASE.md, not a pace.
- **Reframe to calibrant-driven engine self-test.** If the underlying worry is "does the suite engine's fail-fast and per-case probe behave correctly under failure," the calibrant fixtures (BBAAh) are the right substrate — not real ordain cases. But that's arguably already BBAAh's territory or a ₣BC theurge-self-test concern, not BBAA-tier qualification.

Want me to draft a `jjx_drop` or a reslate?

## Character

## Docket
`buh_tt` delegates to `buyy_tt_yawp`, which globs `${BURD_TABTARGET_DIR}/${colophon}.*` when no imprint arg is supplied and picks the first alphabetical match. For imprint-channel colophons with multiple host variants, the first match is arbitrary.

### Concrete trigger
Surfaced during ₢A6AAn. In `Tools/rbk/rbh0/rbhw0_top.sh:52`, the Windows handbook teaches Phase 3 with:
```bash
buh_tt "     Phase 2 (clone repos):              " "${JJZ_FUNDUS_PHASE2}"
```
`JJZ_FUNDUS_PHASE2` is enrolled with channel `imprint`; the tabtargets are `jjw-tfP2.ProvisionPhase2.cerebro.sh` and `jjw-tfP2.ProvisionPhase2.localhost.sh`. The handbook is host-agnostic (it's about "a Windows host" in the abstract), so the glob picks `cerebro` — arbitrary and potentially misleading for a learner setting up a different host.

The prior hardcoded string was `tt/jjw-tfP2.ProvisionPhase2.{host}.sh` — explicit about the placeholder. The `buh_tt` replacement (prescribed in ₢A6AAn's docket) loses that teaching signal in exchange for a clickable link.

### Why this isn't caught by the fixture today
`handbook-render` checks exit 0, not rendered content correctness. The Windows handbook rendering with `cerebro.sh` baked in is a green case — the fixture catches display *breakage* but not display *drift*.

### Candidate directions
- **Suffix annotation idiom**: document the standard `buh_tt ... "${FOO}" "" " (pick your host)"` pattern for imprint-channel colophons in host-agnostic handbooks. Cheapest; no primitive change. Accepts that the rendered link points at an arbitrary variant.
- **Multi-variant rendering in `buyy_tt_yawp`**: when called with no imprint on an imprint-channel colophon, render something like `tt/jjw-tfP2.ProvisionPhase2.{host}.sh` (synthetic placeholder) instead of globbing. Needs a way to detect "colophon is imprint-channel" — likely requires `buz_exec_lookup` or a sibling query on the zipper registry.
- **Require explicit imprint**: make `buh_tt` on an imprint-channel colophon without an imprint arg a hard error. Forces every caller to make a choice. Breaking change; audit scope unclear without a grep sweep.
- **Companion fixture assertion**: extend `handbook-render` (coordinating with ₢A-AAS) to snapshot rendered content and diff against expected per-colophon output. Catches drift, not just breakage. Heavier but pairs naturally with the param1 coverage work.

### Prerequisites
- ₢A6AAn complete — it produced the concrete trigger.
- Likely best sequenced after ₢A-AAS so the fixture's parameterization mechanism is known before deciding whether to leverage it for content-snapshot testing.

### Wrap criterion
- Decision recorded on imprint-channel handling in handbooks (one of the directions above, or another).

- If an idiom only: documented in BCG or the handbook-building guide, and `rbhw0_top.sh:52` updated to apply it.
- Windows handbook (`rbw-hw`) renders in a way that doesn't silently commit a learner to the wrong host variant.

### Notes
- Currently `JJZ_FUNDUS_PHASE2` is the only known imprint-channel colophon used from a host-agnostic handbook. `JJZ_FUNDUS_SCENARIO` and `JJZ_FUNDUS_SINGLE` are also imprint-channel but aren't referenced from handbooks at time of slate.

### http-helper-callsite-port (₢BBAA5) [rough]

## Character

Mechanical DRY port: replace inline curl-with-stderr-capture blocks across the codebase with calls to `rbgu_http_request` (helper landed in BBAAs, lives in `Tools/rbk/rbgu_Utility.sh`). Mount-time discovery — do not assume the surface is what it was when this was slated.

## Discovery recipe

At mount, scan the curl callsites:

```
grep -n 'curl' Tools/rbk/*.sh | grep -v 'rbgu_http_request\|--head\|rbgj'
```

For each hit, decide: compatible or not?

A callsite is **compatible** with the helper when its curl shape matches all of:
- Method-parameterized HTTP request (GET/POST/PUT/DELETE)
- Bearer-token auth passed via a separate token variable
- Accept and Content-Type headers (when present) are `application/json`
- Optional JSON body file via `-d @body`
- Caller wants response body, HTTP status code, and stderr captured to files (rather than discarded or piped)

## Paces

### gauntlet-rerun-infrastructure (₢BBAAr) [rough]

## Character

Verify the iterative-debug surface of the gauntlet suite, sequenced after BBAAg (suite + rbw-tP exist) and before BBAAX (first end-to-end run, green-tally guardrail). BBAAX commits to marshal-zero-between-attempts — without proven single-case rerun against the suite's expensive state-progressing cases (especially §3 ordain, where vessel construction is the costliest step), each debug iteration costs ~1 hour + a GCP project, and the gauntlet's value collapses to "must get it perfect in one shot." That contradicts BBAAX's first-class re-run clause.

Intent-only docket. Code-level planning at mount time.

## Scope

- Verify (or add) `tt/rbtd-s.SingleCase.canonical-onboarding-sequence.sh` handles ordain-* cases against an already-canonical depot, no upstream re-run
- Tamper deliberately — delete a hallmark, blank a regime field, leave a partial reliquary — and verify per-case precondition probes fire with operator-actionable diagnostics naming the expected state and the closest fixture that produces it
- Confirm §4 crucible fixtures (tadmor/moriah/srjcl/pluml) runnable alone against a §3-completed state, no re-ordain required for crucible debug
- Document the iterative debug workflow into the gauntlet runbook surface BBAAI owns: where to look when X fails, what tabtarget to invoke, how to interpret per-case probe output

## Wrap criterion

- Single-case rerun mechanism demonstrably works for ordain-* cases without marshal-zero, against established canonical state
- Per-case probes verified under deliberately-broken state across at least one ordain case and one crucible fixture
- §4 crucible single-fixture rerun confirmed against §3-completed depot
- Debug workflow referenced from BBAAX's docket so the green-tally pace has tooling on hand

## Cost

~30-45 min wall-clock. Reuses canonical state from a prior end-to-end run; does not require its own marshal-zero + GCP project.

## Cross-pace dependencies

- BBAAf landed (canonical-onboarding-sequence fixture + per-case probes exist)
- BBAAg landed (rbw-tP + suite resolver exist)

## Out of scope

- New mechanism design — per-case probe pattern is BBAAf's territory; this pace verifies behavior, not invents it
- BBAAX's first end-to-end green-tally run (uses the tooling proven here)
- Cross-heat debug tooling generalizations

### rbw-tP-first-run (₢BBAAX) [rough]

## Character

First end-to-end execution of the gauntlet suite via rbw-tP against actual fresh state. The bug class this heat exists to catch — silent first-build assumptions — surfaces here in its remaining form: case-level probes have caught what they can during walkthrough and fixture construction; BBAAX exposes the cross-section bugs only the full assembled sequence reveals.

**Wrap criterion is green tally.** Not "ran to completion." Not "captured findings." Not "construction complete." This pace exists as a structural guardrail against the recurring agent failure mode where wrap is driven at "I built the thing" rather than "the thing actually works against reality." If the first run surfaces issues, fixes land here (small) or spawn follow-up paces (large) — but this pace stays open until rbw-tP runs green end-to-end.

Intent-only docket. Triage decisions surface at mount time per surprise.

## Cost

~1 hour wall-clock + 2 GCP projects per attempt (one throwaway from pristine-lifecycle fixture, one canonical from canonical-establish). Re-runs after inline fixes accumulate cost; budget accordingly.

## Scope

- Marshal zero per RELEASE.md §2 (signature commit on HEAD)

  - Tiny inline (one-line bash, message clarity) — fix and re-run within this pace
  - Larger (structural, design call) — slate as follow-up pace, surface to operator before deciding
- Record into pace closure: wall-clock total, per-phase wall-clock, cloud cost, surprises + dispositions

## Constraints

- Honor paddock's Release-branch execution contract

## Character

Final integration pace. Wires pristine-pass as a precondition for `/rbk-prep-release` so the upstream contribution ceremony cannot proceed without a recent pristine green. Closes the loop: pristine qualification becomes load-bearing on release prep, not optional ceremony.

Recency contract is **exact-HEAD-match** — strongest invariant, prevents stale passes. rbw-tP green tally writes an artifact linking pass to HEAD's commit SHA; `/rbk-prep-release` reads it and refuses on mismatch. Artifact is gitignored (deliberately not committed). Artifact mechanism (file location, contents) is a mount-time decision.

Future follow-up to the handbook-render fixture landed in ₣A6 (₢A6AAt). Design decision pace — likely requires some discussion of how to represent per-colophon parameterization in Theurge's test fixtures before any code moves.

## Docket
The handbook-render fixture in ₣A6 exercises 15 of the 16 handbook colophons. The one skipped is `rbw-HWdw` (`RBZ_HW_DOCKER_WSL_NATIVE`), which uses the `param1` channel — it requires a WSL-target argument to run, and the fixture's uniform "invoke and check exit 0" pattern does not supply one.

Decide how the handbook-render fixture should handle this (and any future param1-channel handbooks). Candidate directions:

- **Per-colophon argument lookup**: extend the Rust-side handbook colophon registry with a `default_args` slice per entry. Most entries get `&[]`; `rbw-HWdw` gets a known-safe dummy. Keeps the case loop uniform, localizes the exception.
- **Dedicated subsection in the fixture**: a second section listing only param1 handbooks with their dummy values. Preserves the "one case per colophon" symmetry without threading argument tables through the uniform case.
- **Split fixture**: `handbook-render-simple` (no args) + `handbook-render-parameterized` (args explicit). Heavier and probably not worth it for one offender.

This pace picks a direction, implements it, and extends handbook-render coverage to `rbw-HWdw`.

### Prerequisites
- ₢A6AAt (handbook-render-fixture) must be complete — this pace patches a known scope deferral from that work.

### Wrap criterion
- handbook-render fixture exercises all 16 handbook colophons (including `rbw-HWdw`)
- Pattern generalizes to any future param1 handbooks added via `rbz_zipper.sh`
- Test asserts the chosen dummy param1 value(s) produce a rendering exit (not an argument-missing exit)

### Notes
- Known exit 0 is not the only valid verdict here. The test's purpose is to catch display breakage in handbook rendering — the dummy argument must steer toward a rendered output path, not an early-exit-due-to-bad-arg path.
- `rbw-HWdw` is currently the only param1 handbook. If a second one lands before this pace runs, reassess whether an inline table is still the right shape.

### handbook-render-imprint-coverage (₢BBAAx) [rough]

Drafted from ₢A-AAT in ₣A-.

## Character
Imprint-channel sibling to ₢A-AAS. Design decision pace — the core question is how `buh_tt` and the handbook-render fixture should treat imprint-channel colophons when the handbook is host-agnostic. Likely needs discussion before code lands.

## Docket
`buh_tt` delegates to `buyy_tt_yawp`, which globs `${BURD_TABTARGET_DIR}/${colophon}.*` when no imprint arg is supplied and picks the first alphabetical match. For imprint-channel colophons with multiple host variants, the first match is arbitrary.

### Concrete trigger
Surfaced during ₢A6AAn. In `Tools/rbk/rbh0/rbhw0_top.sh:52`, the Windows handbook teaches Phase 3 with:
```bash
buh_tt "     Phase 2 (clone repos):              " "${JJZ_FUNDUS_PHASE2}"
```
`JJZ_FUNDUS_PHASE2` is enrolled with channel `imprint`; the tabtargets are `jjw-tfP2.ProvisionPhase2.cerebro.sh` and `jjw-tfP2.ProvisionPhase2.localhost.sh`. The handbook is host-agnostic (it's about "a Windows host" in the abstract), so the glob picks `cerebro` — arbitrary and potentially misleading for a learner setting up a different host.

The prior hardcoded string was `tt/jjw-tfP2.ProvisionPhase2.{host}.sh` — explicit about the placeholder. The `buh_tt` replacement (prescribed in ₢A6AAn's docket) loses that teaching signal in exchange for a clickable link.

### Why this isn't caught by the fixture today
`handbook-render` checks exit 0, not rendered content correctness. The Windows handbook rendering with `cerebro.sh` baked in is a green case — the fixture catches display *breakage* but not display *drift*.

### Candidate directions
- **Suffix annotation idiom**: document the standard `buh_tt ... "${FOO}" "" " (pick your host)"` pattern for imprint-channel colophons in host-agnostic handbooks. Cheapest; no primitive change. Accepts that the rendered link points at an arbitrary variant.
- **Multi-variant rendering in `buyy_tt_yawp`**: when called with no imprint on an imprint-channel colophon, render something like `tt/jjw-tfP2.ProvisionPhase2.{host}.sh` (synthetic placeholder) instead of globbing. Needs a way to detect "colophon is imprint-channel" — likely requires `buz_exec_lookup` or a sibling query on the zipper registry.
- **Require explicit imprint**: make `buh_tt` on an imprint-channel colophon without an imprint arg a hard error. Forces every caller to make a choice. Breaking change; audit scope unclear without a grep sweep.
- **Companion fixture assertion**: extend `handbook-render` (coordinating with ₢A-AAS) to snapshot rendered content and diff against expected per-colophon output. Catches drift, not just breakage. Heavier but pairs naturally with the param1 coverage work.

### Prerequisites
- ₢A6AAn complete — it produced the concrete trigger.
- Likely best sequenced after ₢A-AAS so the fixture's parameterization mechanism is known before deciding whether to leverage it for content-snapshot testing.

### Wrap criterion
- Decision recorded on imprint-channel handling in handbooks (one of the directions above, or another).
- If a primitive change: `buh_tt`/`buyy_tt_yawp` updated, call sites audited, fixture updated if behavior is observable.
- If an idiom only: documented in BCG or the handbook-building guide, and `rbhw0_top.sh:52` updated to apply it.
- Windows handbook (`rbw-hw`) renders in a way that doesn't silently commit a learner to the wrong host variant.

### Notes
- Currently `JJZ_FUNDUS_PHASE2` is the only known imprint-channel colophon used from a host-agnostic handbook. `JJZ_FUNDUS_SCENARIO` and `JJZ_FUNDUS_SINGLE` are also imprint-channel but aren't referenced from handbooks at time of slate.
- Pairs thematically with ₢A-AAS — both address the gap where channeled colophons meet uniform handbook/fixture shapes. May be worth groom-time discussion of whether to merge.

### rbtd-fixture-nomenclature-collapse (₢BBAAy) [rough]

## Character

Two-step nomenclature simplification in the rbtd fixture engine.
Mechanical refactor with one careful read of test-coverage impact.

## Goals

1. Eliminate "case name string" as a parallel surface to function name.
   Today `case!()` already sets name from `stringify!($func)`, so case
   names ARE function names — but operator-facing surfaces (commit
   prose, dockets, error messages) treat the kebab form as if it were
   a key. Make canonical = function name; either drop kebab from
   user-facing output entirely, or accept kebab as an alias resolver.

2. Eliminate `rbtdre_Section` abstraction. Replace with flat
   `cases: &[Case]` directly on `rbtdre_Fixture`. Fixture-level
   fail-fast vs keep-going (already controlled by disposition) is the
   only granularity actually wanted. Multi-section fixtures
   (enrollment-validation, regime-validation) absorb their grouping
   into case-name prefixes if helpful for display.

## Wrap criterion

- All sections collapsed; `rbtdre_Section` struct removed.
- `calibrant-fail-fast` inter-section coverage retired or repurposed
  (it currently exists to test the abstraction being removed).
- All fixtures still pass under their existing tabtargets.

## Out of scope

- Renaming individual case functions for prose readability.
- Changes to fixture lifecycle (setup/teardown), dispositions,
  or run/list tabtarget surfaces.
- Anything in rbtw or upstream of the engine API.

### single-case-imprint-collapse (₢BBAA1) [rough]

## Character

Tabtarget-surface refactor. Mechanical except for one judgment call:
the colophon name and channel.

## Goals

Replace the imprint-channel `rbtd-s.FixtureCase.<fixture>.sh [case]`
pattern with a single non-imprint, two-param colophon: param1 is the
fixture name, param2 is the rust test case function name (e.g.
`rbtdrf_rv_rbrv_all_vessels`). The new tabtarget covers ALL
manifest-registered fixtures, not just crucible ones.

The tabtarget is self-documenting — invocation with missing or invalid
arguments fails with a usage listing instead of an opaque error:

- No params: list all valid param1 values (fixture names from the
  manifest); exit nonzero.
- Invalid param1: same — list all valid param1 values; exit nonzero.
- Valid param1, missing or invalid param2: list valid param2 values
  (case names registered for that fixture); exit nonzero.
- Both valid: run the case.

## Wrap criterion

- Single new tabtarget routes by fixture-name lookup against the
  rbtd manifest; rejects unknown fixture names.
- Helpful-failure UX: missing/invalid args produce a list of valid
  values for the offending position and a nonzero exit.
- Per-fixture FixtureCase.<fixture>.sh files retired.
- Crucible-fixture single-case semantics preserved: still operates on
  already-charged state, never charges/quenches.
- BUK channel discipline honored — the new colophon enrolls under the
  param1 channel, not imprint; zipper updated; CLAUDE.md tabtarget table
  reflects the change.
- Fast suite green; canonical onboarding-sequence cases unchanged.

## Out of scope

- FixtureRun (whole-fixture) tabtargets — unchanged.
- Charge/quench tabtargets — unchanged.
- Adding new fixtures or new manifest fields.

## References

- Tools/buk/buk-claude-context.md — channel semantics (imprint vs param1)
- BBAAz onboarding-sequence-buildout pattern — precedent for
  manifest-driven dispatch
- rbtd manifest: rbtd/src/rbtdrm_manifest.rs

### http-helper-callsite-port (₢BBAA5) [rough]

## Character

Mechanical DRY port: replace inline curl-with-stderr-capture blocks across the codebase with calls to `rbgu_http_request` (helper landed in BBAAs, lives in `Tools/rbk/rbgu_Utility.sh`). Mount-time discovery — do not assume the surface is what it was when this was slated.

## Discovery recipe

At mount, scan the curl callsites:

```
grep -n 'curl' Tools/rbk/*.sh | grep -v 'rbgu_http_request\|--head\|rbgj'
```

For each hit, decide: compatible or not?

A callsite is **compatible** with the helper when its curl shape matches all of:
- Method-parameterized HTTP request (GET/POST/PUT/DELETE)
- Bearer-token auth passed via a separate token variable
- Accept and Content-Type headers (when present) are `application/json`
- Optional JSON body file via `-d @body`
- Caller wants response body, HTTP status code, and stderr captured to files (rather than discarded or piped)

A callsite is **incompatible** (leave inline; possibly sibling-helper in a future pass) when any of:
- HEAD-only existence probe (`--head`)
- Form-encoded body (`application/x-www-form-urlencoded`)
- Runs in a different shell environment (e.g., alpine sh inside Cloud Build step scripts)
- Other Content-Type or unusual header set the helper doesn't speak

## Wrap criterion

- Every callsite found by the recipe has been classified compatible or incompatible
- Every compatible callsite calls the helper instead of inlining the curl flags
- No new inline curl block matching the compatible shape has appeared in the working tree
- `tt/rbtd-b.Build.sh` green; `tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.fast.sh` baseline preserved
- At least one empirical run exercises a ported callsite end-to-end

## Cross-pace dependencies

- BBAAs landed (helper available)

## Out of scope

- Tolerance/timeout tuning of any retry or polling loop
- New helper variants (form-encoded, HEAD-predicate, etc.) — those are separate paces if warranted
- Restructuring caller-side retry/policy logic — only the curl-flag block is portable; the loop around it stays as-is

content.text:
Yes — there's already a pace that IS the end-to-end release test. ₢BBAAr isn't needed; what's needed is to clear it out of the way and let ₢BBAAX run.

## Remaining pace inventory

| Coronet | Silks | RELEASE.md alignment |
|---------|-------|--------------------|
| ₢BBAAr | gauntlet-rerun-infrastructure | **Inverted** — proposes patch-and-continue debug surface; contradicts step-3 "return to step 2 — never patch-and-continue" |
| **₢BBAAX** | **rbw-tP-first-run** | **Direct** — wrap criterion IS green tally on `rbw-tP`; explicitly cites RELEASE.md failure-mode contract |
| ₢BBAAH | prep-release-integration | Wires pristine-pass as precondition to `/rbk-prep-release` (step 4 of RELEASE.md) |
| ₢BBAAI | release-doc-revision | Maintenance read of RELEASE.md after the heat lands |
| ₢BBAAq | rbs0-sync-sweep-from-bb-changes | Spec hygiene tail-pace |
| ₢BBAAt | charge-folds-readiness-wait | Engine cleanup, unrelated to release ceremony |
| ₢BBAAw | handbook-render-param1-coverage | Drafted from ₣A- — coverage extension |
| ₢BBAAx | handbook-render-imprint-coverage | Drafted from ₣A- — coverage extension |
| ₢BBAAy | rbtd-fixture-nomenclature-collapse | Engine refactor |
| ₢BBAA1 | single-case-imprint-collapse | Tabtarget refactor |
| ₢BBAA5 | http-helper-callsite-port | DRY sweep |

## ₢BBAAX is exactly what you're asking for

Re-reading its docket against RELEASE.md cold:

- **Wrap criterion is green tally** — restated three times in the docket as a guardrail against agent-drift
- Scope walks RELEASE.md verbatim: "Marshal zero per RELEASE.md §2 (signature commit on HEAD)" → "Run rbw-tP, which enforces entry contract and exec's the gauntlet suite tabtarget"
- Constraints clause: "Per RELEASE.md failure mode contract: mid-qualification failure means start over from marshal-zero, not patch-and-continue"
- Triage policy: tiny inline fixes happen within the pace; larger findings spawn follow-up paces (the standard discovery-shape for a first-real-run pace)
- Dependencies satisfied: BBAAg (gauntlet-suite-assembly) landed, so `rbw-tP` and the gauntlet suite tabtarget exist

## Proposal

Two structural moves. Both reversible.
